passages.explanation: The chapter opens with Dorian's growing paranoia about his servant Victor, studying his face for signs of suspicion. This surveillance of others marks a new phase in Dorian's corruption: the secret of the portrait transforms every relationship into a potential threat. The mirror and the screen become opposing symbols, one reflecting his unchanged beauty, the other hiding his true self. Wilde shows how secrets breed isolation and distrust.

The exchange with Mrs. Leaf about the old schoolroom is rich with symbolism. The dusty, abandoned room where Dorian spent his childhood will now house his corruption, a perfect inversion of innocence. The housekeeper's fussing domesticity contrasts sharply with the dark purpose Dorian has in mind. His wince at the mention of his grandfather hints at a legacy of cruelty that predates Lord Henry's influence.

The purple-and-gold Venetian coverlet that Dorian uses to wrap the portrait is described as a pall for the dead, and Wilde makes the metaphor explicit: the portrait carries 'a corruption of its own, worse than the corruption of death.' Dorian's brief regret that he did not confide in Basil is his last flicker of moral clarity. His recognition that Basil's love 'had nothing in it that was not noble' makes his rejection of that love all the more tragic. Wilde suggests that redemption was available but consciously refused.

The scene with Mr. Hubbard the frame-maker provides darkly comic relief while also showing Dorian's practiced skill at deception. His charm works on everyone, the frame-maker is 'delighted to be of any service,' and Wilde suggests that beauty itself is a form of manipulation. The mundane logistics of moving a portrait upstairs take on the weight of a body being hidden, blurring the line between aesthetic arrangement and criminal concealment.

The old schoolroom is the novel's most powerful symbolic space. Dorian's memories of childhood innocence, the toy chest where he hid as a boy, the dog-eared schoolbooks, collide with the present horror of what he is storing there. The tapestry of a king and queen playing chess suggests lives governed by strategy and manipulation. Wilde makes us feel the full weight of lost innocence: the room that once sheltered a child will now shelter a record of his sins.

Dorian's brief fantasy that his nature might 'grow finer' and the portrait might heal is immediately contradicted by his own reasoning about the inevitability of aging on the canvas. His protectiveness over the portrait, nearly ready to physically attack Mr. Hubbard for wanting to look, shows how the secret has already begun to deform his behavior. The locked door and pocketed key complete the architecture of the double life.

The anxiety about Victor reading the newspaper clipping about Sibyl's inquest reveals how Dorian's secret has turned his home into a surveillance state. His paranoia about servants, letters, and overheard conversations reflects Wilde's understanding that the double life requires constant vigilance. The tearing up of the newspaper is a futile attempt to destroy evidence of consequence, echoing his broader project of separating action from accountability.

The yellow book, widely understood as a stand-in for Huysmans' Against Nature, becomes Lord Henry's most potent instrument of corruption. Its description as 'poisonous,' with sins 'passing in dumb show' and a style that merges mystical philosophy with sensual confession, mirrors exactly what the novel itself is doing to Dorian. The book absorbs him so completely he loses track of time, suggesting that aesthetic experience can become a kind of narcotic that replaces moral consciousness entirely.

The chapter's final exchange is deceptively simple but thematically crucial. Dorian's distinction between liking the book and being fascinated by it echoes the novel's larger question about whether beauty and art must be good to be powerful. Lord Henry's knowing 'Ah, you have discovered that?' confirms he sent the book as a deliberate act of corruption, a textual Lord Henry that will continue working on Dorian even in Henry's absence. The passage into the dining room closes the chapter with the suggestion that consumption, of food, of art, of experience, has become Dorian's defining mode of existence.
